topic: A person runs into their ex at a mutual friend's wedding
raw_output:
[story 1]

The ballroom was a dizzying swirl of satin and forced merriment. I clutched my champagne flute so tightly my knuckles were white, the crystal digging into my palm. Then, across the crowded dance floor, a familiar silhouette materialized. It was him. My ex. A sudden, sharp intake of breath stole the air from my lungs. Every muscle in my body seemed to lock up, a cold, heavy pressure settling in my chest. I immediately pivoted, pretending to be deeply engrossed in a conversation with a distant acquaintance, my gaze fixed on the intricate pattern of the rug beneath my feet, desperate to melt into the shadows. The sound of his laughter, bright and carefree, seemed amplified in the sudden silence of my own mind. I needed an escape route, a corner, anything to dissolve this intensely unwelcome recognition.

[story 2]

“Oh, wow, look who it is!” Sarah’s voice cut through the din, laced with genuine delight. My stomach performed a sudden, violent flip. I froze mid-sip, the liquid sloshing dangerously close to the rim of the glass. There he was, standing near the buffet, looking impossibly composed. My breath hitched, shallow and rapid, like a trapped bird beating against ribs. I felt a distinct urge to simply vanish, to become invisible. I started moving, a jerky, unnatural pace toward the periphery of the room, my eyes darting everywhere but his. Each step felt monumental, weighted with a profound need to retreat. My hands were damp; I focused intensely on smoothing a nonexistent wrinkle on my dress, anything to mask the tremor running through my limbs.

[story 3]

The music swelled, a vibrant, relentless rhythm that seemed to mock the sudden, icy stillness that had gripped me. I watched from across the room as he approached, and the world narrowed instantly to the space between us. My vision tunneled slightly. A strange numbness began to spread from my core outward, making the bright lights of the celebration feel harsh and overwhelming. I forced a smile, a brittle, paper-thin thing that didn't reach my eyes. My throat felt constricted, as if a thick band had been placed around it. I took a deliberate, slow sip of water, trying to anchor myself to the present moment, but the air around him felt suddenly thin, charged with a history I desperately wanted to keep buried. I subtly shifted my weight, wanting nothing more than to be anywhere else but this epicenter of unwelcome memory.
